turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Thomas has our opinion this morning in case 16-1362, Encino Motorcars v. Navarro.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: This case comes to us on a Writ of Certiorari to the United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit. Respondents are current and former service advisors of petitioner Encino Motors, a car dealership in California. The service advisors sued the dealership for failing to pay them overtime under the Fair Labor Standards Act. The dealership argued that service advisors are not entitled to overtime pay under the Act, which exempts "any salesman, partsman, or mechanic primarily engaged in selling or servicing automobiles, trucks, or farm implements." The District Court agreed that service advisors are exempt and dismiss the suit. But the Ninth Circuit reversed. It deferred to a 2011 Department of Labor rule that interpreted the exemption to exclude service advisors. Two terms ago, this court vacated the Ninth Circuit's judgment. We held that the Department of Labor's rule is procedurally defective and we remanded for the Ninth Circuit to interpret the exemption without deference to the agency. On remand, the Ninth Circuit again held that the exemption excludes service advisors. In an opinion filed with the clerk today, we reverse. We hold that service advisors are exempt from the overtime pay requirement because they are salesman primarily engaged in servicing automobiles. Service advisors are salesman. They sell customer services for their vehicles. Service advisors are also primarily engaged in servicing automobiles. They are integral to the servicing process. While service advisors do not spend most of their time physically repairing automobiles, neither do partsman who the parties agree are primarily engaged in servicing automobiles. The Ninth Circuit invoked the principle that exemptions to the Fair Labor Standards Act should be construed narrowly. We reject this principle. Statutory exemptions should be construed fairly, not narrowly. The Ninth Circuit also noted that the legislative history does not mention service advisors but silence in the legislative history is not a useful tool for interpreting statutes. For these reasons and others set forth in our opinion, we reverse the judgment of the Ninth Circuit. Justice Ginsburg has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justices Breyer, Sotomayor and Kagan have joined.
